name: Budha
description: Budha, in Hindu mythology, is the deity associated with the planet Mercury and Wednesday. He is considered the son of Chandra (the Moon) and Tara (the wife of Brihaspati, the guru of the gods). His birth story varies, with some accounts detailing a celestial abduction and others a willing union. Budha is not to be confused with the Buddha, the founder of Buddhism. He is depicted as a handsome, mild-mannered god, often riding a chariot or a lion. Budha is revered as the god of merchandise, protector of merchants, and patron of knowledge, wisdom, and communication. He is also associated with intelligence, wit, and eloquence. He is believed to bestow these qualities upon his devotees. He governs trade, commerce, education, and writing. Budha is generally considered a benevolent deity, although his influence can sometimes be associated with trickery or instability due to his connection with the ever-changing nature of Mercury. He is often invoked for success in business, academic pursuits, and communication skills. He is worshipped for clarity of mind, good fortune, and protection from mental ailments. He is also linked to green color and the element of earth. Budha's worship is believed to mitigate the negative effects of Mercury in one's astrological chart, bringing balance and harmony to life.
subtitle: God of Mercury, Intellect, and Commerce

keyMyths.title: Birth of Budha
keyMyths.description: Budha's birth is shrouded in mystery, born from the union of Chandra and Tara. This union, whether consensual or not, led to a celestial dispute and the birth of the intelligent Budha.

keyMyths.title: Budha's Marriage
keyMyths.description: Budha is married to Ila, who is sometimes described as a male prince transformed into a woman. Their union symbolizes the balance of masculine and feminine energies.

keyMyths.title: Budha's Influence on Communication
keyMyths.description: Devotees believe that worshipping Budha enhances their communication skills and intellect. He blesses his followers with wisdom and eloquence in speech and writing.
